SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments use two real gaze-annotated datasets and compare the proposed text+gaze CNN against prior handcrafted-gaze systems and several text-only CNN variants, providing a basic ablation that supports the main claims. The paper reports limits (e.g., only a 1.36% gaze gain for sarcasm and fixed hyper-parameters), and related work coverage is adequate. However, sensitivity analyses and hyper-parameter tuning are limited, statistical significance is only partially discussed, and code is not mentioned (reproducibility relies mainly on reported hyper-parameters), so important experimental depth is missing. | B(3): The paper runs experiments on two published datasets and compares multiple CNN variants (text-only vs text+gaze) against prior handcrafted-feature baselines, which provides a reasonable empirical basis for the main claims. However, sensitivity analyses and deeper ablations are limited (hyperparameters fixed by trial-and-error, minimal analysis of why gaze adds little for sarcasm beyond noting a vocabulary confound), and only accuracy is reported. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations (insignificant gaze gain for sarcasm, hyperparameter tuning left for future work) are discussed, but reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters reported but code and detailed gaze-processing/release information are not mentioned).

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments use two real gaze-annotated datasets and compare the proposed text+gaze CNN against prior handcrafted-gaze systems and several text-only CNN variants, providing a basic ablation that supports the main claims. The paper reports limits (e.g., only a 1.36% gaze gain for sarcasm and fixed hyper-parameters), and related work coverage is adequate. However, sensitivity analyses and hyper-parameter tuning are limited, statistical significance is only partially discussed, and code is not mentioned (reproducibility relies mainly on reported hyper-parameters), so important experimental depth is missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs experiments on two published datasets and compares multiple CNN variants (text-only vs text+gaze) against prior handcrafted-feature baselines, which provides a reasonable empirical basis for the main claims. However, sensitivity analyses and deeper ablations are limited (hyperparameters fixed by trial-and-error, minimal analysis of why gaze adds little for sarcasm beyond noting a vocabulary confound), and only accuracy is reported. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations (insignificant gaze gain for sarcasm, hyperparameter tuning left for future work) are discussed, but reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters reported but code and detailed gaze-processing/release information are not mentioned).

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles an important problem (sentiment and sarcasm detection) and introduces a promising approach to learn cognitive features from gaze using CNNs, showing concrete gains (text+gaze CNN outperforms text-only and handcrafted-feature baselines; CNN variants beat prior sarcasm systems by up to 11.27%). However, the incremental benefit of adding gaze for sarcasm is small (only 1.36%, reported as statistically insignificant), and experiments use small datasets with few readers (994 and 843 snippets; gaze from 7 and 5 readers), limiting immediate practical impact. Overall, the work moderately advances the area by demonstrating feasibility and suggesting new directions for integrating cognitive signals, but is unlikely to be transformative without larger-scale validation or more substantial gains. | B(3): The paper tackles an interesting and relevant problem—learning cognitive features from gaze for sentiment and sarcasm detection—and demonstrates that a text+gaze CNN can outperform text-only and handcrafted-feature systems on the provided benchmarks. However, datasets are small (994 and 843 snippets with eye-tracking from only 5–7 readers) and the gaze component gives only marginal gains for sarcasm (a 1.36% improvement, reported as statistically insignificant), limiting immediate practical impact. The approach does open a promising direction (automatic learning of cognitive features from raw gaze signals) that could influence future work if validated on larger, more diverse data. Overall, the contribution is useful and potentially influential but not yet transformative given the limited empirical scope and modest gains.

However, the incremental benefit of adding gaze for sarcasm is small (only 1.36%, reported as statistically insignificant), and experiments use small datasets with few readers (994 and 843 snippets; gaze from 7 and 5 readers), limiting immediate practical impact. Overall, the work moderately advances the area by demonstrating feasibility and suggesting new directions for integrating cognitive signals, but is unlikely to be transformative without larger-scale validation or more substantial gains.

The approach does open a promising direction (automatic learning of cognitive features from raw gaze signals) that could influence future work if validated on larger, more diverse data. Overall, the contribution is useful and potentially influential but not yet transformative given the limited empirical scope and modest gains.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper compares against appropriate baselines (Mishra et al. 2016 systems using handcrafted text and gaze features) and multiple CNN text-only variants, using the same datasets and the same accuracy metric. It explicitly acknowledges when adding gaze produced only a small, statistically insignificant improvement for sarcasm and analyses possible reasons (e.g., vocabulary differences), and discusses CNN vs. handcrafted-feature differences. Related work coverage is reported as adequate, so comparisons are comprehensive and fair. | B(4): The paper compares against relevant prior work (Mishra et al. systems for sentiment/sarcasm) and strong internal baselines (text-only CNN variants) using the same datasets and accuracy metric. The comparisons are presented fairly and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably (e.g., the 1.36% insignificant gain from adding gaze for sarcasm) and analyze reasons (vocabulary differences, hyperparameter tuning). One minor limitation is that the comparisons appear focused on the cited eye-tracking/handcrafted-feature papers and internal CNN variants, so broader recent deep-learning baselines outside those specific works are not evident from the summary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate baselines (Mishra et al. 2016 systems using handcrafted text and gaze features) and multiple CNN text-only variants, using the same datasets and the same accuracy metric. It explicitly acknowledges when adding gaze produced only a small, statistically insignificant improvement for sarcasm and analyses possible reasons (e.g., vocabulary differences), and discusses CNN vs. handcrafted-feature differences. Related work coverage is reported as adequate, so comparisons are comprehensive and fair.

The comparisons are presented fairly and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably (e.g., the 1.36% insignificant gain from adding gaze for sarcasm) and analyze reasons (vocabulary differences, hyperparameter tuning). One minor limitation is that the comparisons appear focused on the cited eye-tracking/handcrafted-feature papers and internal CNN variants, so broader recent deep-learning baselines outside those specific works are not evident from the summary.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main experimental claims are supported by comparative results against prior handcrafted-feature systems and CNN text-only baselines, and the authors acknowledge the small (994 and 843 snippets) datasets and limited reader samples (7 and 5), plus a reported statistically insignificant 1.36% gaze benefit for sarcasm. However, methodology has minor weaknesses: reliance on accuracy alone, hyper-parameters set by trial-and-error, and limited reporting of statistical tests (only the insignificant sarcasm gain is explicitly mentioned), which constrain the strength and generalizability of the conclusions. Overall the technical approach and reported experiments are sound, but the limited data and some incomplete experimental controls prevent a full, unqualified validation of all claims. | B(3): The paper’s main claims are backed by experimental results showing CNN text+gaze models outperform text-only and some handcrafted-gaze baselines, and the authors honestly report the small (1.36%) and statistically insignificant gaze gain for sarcasm. There are no mathematical derivations to verify. However, the experimental methodology has weaknesses: small datasets (994 and 843 snippets) with gaze from only 7 and 5 readers, evaluation reported only in accuracy, limited detail on statistical testing, and hyperparameters set by trial-and-error, which undermines confidence in the robustness of the claims. Overall the work is promising and partially supported, but methodological limitations and some unsupported generalization claims justify a cautious score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main experimental claims are supported by comparative results against prior handcrafted-feature systems and CNN text-only baselines, and the authors acknowledge the small (994 and 843 snippets) datasets and limited reader samples (7 and 5), plus a reported statistically insignificant 1.36% gaze benefit for sarcasm. However, methodology has minor weaknesses: reliance on accuracy alone, hyper-parameters set by trial-and-error, and limited reporting of statistical tests (only the insignificant sarcasm gain is explicitly mentioned), which constrain the strength and generalizability of the conclusions. Overall the technical approach and reported experiments are sound, but the limited data and some incomplete experimental controls prevent a full, unqualified validation of all claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are backed by experimental results showing CNN text+gaze models outperform text-only and some handcrafted-gaze baselines, and the authors honestly report the small (1.36%) and statistically insignificant gaze gain for sarcasm. There are no mathematical derivations to verify. However, the experimental methodology has weaknesses: small datasets (994 and 843 snippets) with gaze from only 7 and 5 readers, evaluation reported only in accuracy, limited detail on statistical testing, and hyperparameters set by trial-and-error, which undermines confidence in the robustness of the claims. Overall the work is promising and partially supported, but methodological limitations and some unsupported generalization claims justify a cautious score.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The topic—sentiment and sarcasm detection augmented with eye-tracking/gaze data—is squarely within NLP and of interest to top NLP venues, and the paper uses standard ML approaches (CNNs, pre-trained word2vec) and compares against relevant baselines. However, the work relies on small datasets (994 and 843 snippets with gaze from only 5–7 readers), reports only accuracy and modest/insignificant gains from the gaze component (e.g., 1.36% for sarcasm), and lacks code mention; these scope and evaluation limitations make it a good fit but with minor concerns about broad impact for a top-tier ML venue. | B(4): This paper addresses a clearly NLP-relevant topic (integrating eye-tracking/gaze signals with text for sentiment and sarcasm classification) and proposes a neural (CNN) approach that compares against prior handcrafted-gaze systems and text-only CNN baselines, so it targets the right audience. Evaluation follows community norms (comparisons to prior work, reporting accuracy and hyperparameters) and datasets are appropriate for the task (Dataset 1: 994 snippets, 7 readers; Dataset 2: 843 snippets, 5 readers), though the small size and limited improvement for sarcasm (+1.36% reported as insignificant) raise generalizability and ML-impact concerns. Overall this is a good fit for a top NLP venue (multimodal/cognitive-NLP), with minor scope concerns for a general ML conference.

However, the work relies on small datasets (994 and 843 snippets with gaze from only 5–7 readers), reports only accuracy and modest/insignificant gains from the gaze component (e.g., 1.36% for sarcasm), and lacks code mention; these scope and evaluation limitations make it a good fit but with minor concerns about broad impact for a top-tier ML venue.

Evaluation follows community norms (comparisons to prior work, reporting accuracy and hyperparameters) and datasets are appropriate for the task (Dataset 1: 994 snippets, 7 readers; Dataset 2: 843 snippets, 5 readers), though the small size and limited improvement for sarcasm (+1.36% reported as insignificant) raise generalizability and ML-impact concerns. Overall this is a good fit for a top NLP venue (multimodal/cognitive-NLP), with minor scope concerns for a general ML conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper’s primary novelty is applying a CNN framework to raw eye-movement/gaze data to automatically learn cognitive features and combining these with text, which clearly differentiates it from prior work that relied on handcrafted gaze/text features (Mishra et al.). However, sentiment and sarcasm classification are well-studied problems and the approach is a relatively straightforward adaptation of existing CNN techniques to a new modality. Empirical gains are modest (e.g., only a 1.36% and statistically insignificant improvement for sarcasm when adding gaze), suggesting the contribution is an incremental but useful extension rather than a highly original breakthrough. | B(3): The paper applies convolutional neural networks to raw gaze/eye-movement data to automatically learn cognitive features, which is a novel application relative to prior work that relied on handcrafted text and gaze features (Mishra et al.). However, the core technical novelty is mainly the application/combination of existing CNN methods to gaze signals rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea, and reported gains are modest (e.g., only a 1.36% non-significant improvement for sarcasm). The authors do clearly differentiate their approach from prior handcrafted-feature systems, making this a solid but incremental contribution that would inform but not greatly surprise experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s primary novelty is applying a CNN framework to raw eye-movement/gaze data to automatically learn cognitive features and combining these with text, which clearly differentiates it from prior work that relied on handcrafted gaze/text features (Mishra et al.). However, sentiment and sarcasm classification are well-studied problems and the approach is a relatively straightforward adaptation of existing CNN techniques to a new modality. Empirical gains are modest (e.g., only a 1.36% and statistically insignificant improvement for sarcasm when adding gaze), suggesting the contribution is an incremental but useful extension rather than a highly original breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies convolutional neural networks to raw gaze/eye-movement data to automatically learn cognitive features, which is a novel application relative to prior work that relied on handcrafted text and gaze features (Mishra et al.). However, the core technical novelty is mainly the application/combination of existing CNN methods to gaze signals rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea, and reported gains are modest (e.g., only a 1.36% non-significant improvement for sarcasm). The authors do clearly differentiate their approach from prior handcrafted-feature systems, making this a solid but incremental contribution that would inform but not greatly surprise experts.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear experimental narrative (methodology, datasets, baselines, evaluation) and its main contributions are stated up front (CNN framework for joint text+gaze features for sentiment/sarcasm). Notation and methodological description appear consistent and sufficiently detailed (two-component CNN, pre-trained embeddings, convolution on gaze inputs) and hyperparameters are reported. Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is adequate; minor presentation issues include lack of mention of code availability and that some hyper-parameter choices were fixed by trial-and-error (left for future work), which could have been discussed more fully. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear methodological progression (two-component CNN, datasets, baselines, evaluation) and explicit reporting of hyperparameters and limitations, suggesting a logical flow from introduction to experiments and conclusion. The main contributions are stated early (automatically learning cognitive features from gaze and improved classification vs. text-only and handcrafted baselines). Notation and experimental reporting seem consistent and well-defined (datasets, metrics, baselines are clearly described), and related work coverage is adequate. The summary does not specify the presence or quality of figures/tables/examples, so their effectiveness is unclear, but overall writing quality is reported as good.

main_claims: Introduces a CNN-based framework that automatically learns cognitive features from eye-movement/gaze data and textual input for sentiment polarity and sarcasm detection.
The combined text+gaze CNN yields better classification performance than (i) CNNs using text input alone and (ii) existing systems that rely on handcrafted text and gaze features.

For sarcasm detection, CNN variants outperform traditional systems by up to 11.27%; however, adding the gaze component to the CNN produces only a 1.36% improvement, which is reported as statistically insignificant.
Argues that CNNs are suitable for learning filters on eye-movement data that can capture cognitive patterns (e.g., long-distance saccades) analogous to image filters used in vision tasks.
_summary.methodology: A two-component Convolutional Neural Network processes text (using pre-trained word2vec embeddings) and raw gaze/eye-movement inputs (with 2D convolutional filters) to automatically learn feature representations for joint classification of sentiment polarity and sarcasm.
datasets: Dataset 1 (Mishra et al., 2016a) — 994 snippets: 383 positive, 611 negative; 350 sarcastic; eye-movement data from 7 readers.
Dataset 2 (Joshi et al., 2014) — 843 snippets (movie reviews and normalized tweets): 443 positive, 400 negative; eye-movement data from 5 readers.
baselines: Mishra et al. (2016c) — systems using handcrafted text and gaze features (sentiment).
Mishra et al. (2016b) — sarcasm classifier (sarcasm detection).
CNN variants using text input alone (STATICTEXT, NONSTATICTEXT, MULTICHANNELTEXT).
limitations: Adding gaze to the CNN produced only a 1.36% improvement for sarcasm detection, which is reported as statistically insignificant.
Hyper-parameters were fixed by trial and error and authors state that tuning might improve performance (left for future work).

In the sarcasm dataset a clear vocabulary difference between sarcasm and non-sarcasm classes may have allowed the text component alone to capture the distinction, limiting the added value of gaze features.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - sentiment analysis and sarcasm detection using eye-tracking/gaze data
